lesson.menu_desc: Airport situations, reported speech & a reading on travel disruptions -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Smooth Travels: At the Airport
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 6 -- Smooth Travels: At the Airport

paras: It was supposed to be the easiest leg of the whole trip: a short connecting flight from Frankfurt to Lisbon, the last hop before a week by the sea. Marta had cleared security with two hours to spare, found a quiet corner of the departure lounge, and allowed herself to relax. Then the board flickered. Her flight had moved from "On time" to "Delayed", and a few minutes later to a word every traveller dreads: "Cancelled".

At the service desk, the ground staff were calm but honest. The agent explained that the flight had been overbooked and then grounded by a technical fault, and that there was simply no spare aircraft that night. She told Marta she would be rebooked onto the first morning flight, and warned her that a handful of passengers were still on standby. Marta asked about her suitcase; the agent said it had already been tagged through and would be waiting at baggage claim in Lisbon, whatever happened to her.

What could have ruined the evening became, oddly, one of the better memories of the trip. The airline handed out vouchers for a nearby hotel, and Marta found herself sharing a late dinner with three strangers who had all been bumped from the same flight. By the time she finally landed the next day, jet lag and all, the story she told her friends was not about the cancellation at all. It was about the people she had met because of it.

reading.prompt: What is the best title for this reading?
choices: How airlines decide which flights to cancel
A cancelled flight that turned into a good memory
A guide to getting compensation from airlines

items: Marta arrived at the gate at the last minute, with no time to spare.
f
She had cleared security two hours early and was relaxing in the departure lounge.
The agent said the flight had been overbooked and then grounded by a technical fault.
t
Both reasons are given at the service desk.
Marta's suitcase was left behind in Frankfurt because of the cancellation.
f
The agent said it had been tagged through and would wait at baggage claim in Lisbon.
In the end, the story Marta told her friends focused on the people she met, not the cancellation.
t
The last paragraph says the story was about the strangers, not the cancelled flight.

matching.title: Match each airport expression to its meaning
defs: the tired, out-of-sync feeling after flying across time zones
a waiting status where you only fly if a seat becomes free
the wait at an airport between one flight and the next
the area with belts where you collect checked luggage after landing
when an airline has sold more seats than the aircraft has
to be taken off a flight you had booked, usually because it was full

guiding.title: Talk about it
items: Has a delay or cancellation ever changed your trip for the better? What happened?
Marta stayed calm and the staff were honest. Do you stay calm when travel goes wrong, or do you get stressed?
Would you accept a voucher and a later flight if your flight were overbooked? Why or why not?
What is the kindest thing an airline or a stranger has ever done for you while travelling?

rephrase.title: Report each sentence with said / told + backshift
items: "Your flight is cancelled."  (the agent / told)
told
"We will rebook you on the morning flight."  (the staff / said)
said
"Your suitcase has already been tagged through."  (she / told)
told
"A few passengers are still on standby."  (the agent / said)
said
"I can give you a hotel voucher."  (he / told)
told

answerkey.title: Reveal the reported answers
list: The agent told me (that) my flight was cancelled.
The staff said (that) they would rebook me on the morning flight.
She told me (that) my suitcase had already been tagged through.
The agent said (that) a few passengers were still on standby.
He told me (that) he could give me a hotel voucher.
answerkey.note: Backshift the tense one step back: present (is) → past (was); will → would; present perfect (has been) → past perfect (had been); can → could. Use told + person (told me) but said with no person (said that...).

listenings.text: Good afternoon, passengers. This is an important announcement for travellers on the four-fifteen service to Lisbon. We are sorry to inform you that your departure has been delayed by two hours due to the late arrival of the incoming aircraft. Passengers with a connecting flight should speak to the ground staff at the desk near gate twelve, who will help you rebook your onward journey at no extra charge. The departure lounge has been moved from the main hall to the upper level, just past security. Please keep your boarding pass and travel documents ready, and listen for further announcements. We thank you for your patience and we apologise for any inconvenience.

Honestly, what should have been a simple trip turned into a small adventure. At the desk, the agent told me the flight was overbooked, and she asked whether I would take a later one in exchange for a voucher. I said I would, since I had a long layover anyway. Then she warned me that my bag might be on standby and that I ought to check baggage claim carefully. In the end, the airline misplaced nothing, the connecting flight was smooth, and I even fast-tracked through customs. So now, whenever a trip goes wrong at first, I just tell myself it will probably make a better story later.

extra_audio.text: First, she checked in and heard that her flight had been overbooked. Then an agent told her she had been bumped but would be rebooked. After clearing security, she waited in the departure lounge for her connecting flight. At baggage claim, the ground staff said the airline had misplaced her suitcase. Finally, exhausted by jet lag, she cleared customs and went straight to bed.
